arguments.new_str:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net472</TargetFramework>
    <Description>A legacy-style project for modernization testing</Description>
    <Company>TestCo</Company>
    <Copyright>Copyright © 2024</Copyright>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture of the legacy project. Let me identify all the legacy patterns and modernize the project file.

Legacy patterns found:
1. **Old-style `<Project>` element** — uses `ToolsVersion` and `xmlns` instead of SDK attribute
2. **Explicit `Microsoft.Common.props` import** — handled automatically by SDK
3. **Boilerplate `Configuration`/`Platform` properties** — SDK defaults handle these
4. **`ProjectGuid`** — not needed in SDK-style
5. **`TargetFrameworkVersion` (v4.7.2)** — should use `TargetFramework` with moniker `net472`


6. **`FileAlignment`**, **`AutoGenerateBindingRedirects`**, and **`Deterministic`** — all defaults in SDK-style projects
7. **Debug/Release property groups** — SDK provides sensible defaults for debug symbols, optimization, output paths, and warning levels
8. **Explicit framework references and compile includes** — automatically handled by the SDK

's globbing system
9. **`Microsoft.CSharp.targets` import and `Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs`** — both unnecessary; the SDK manages these, and assembly attributes can be auto-generated and configured directly in the project file

Now I'm modernizing the csproj to remove legacy cruft and consolidating the assembly metadata...

Modernizing the project file...

  </ItemGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include="Calculator.cs" />
    <Compile Include="StringHelper.cs" />
    <Compile Include="Properties\AssemblyInfo.cs" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <Import Project="$(MSBuildToolsPath)\Microsoft.CSharp.targets" />
</Project>

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net472</TargetFramework>
    <Description>A legacy-style project for modernization testing</Description>
    <Company>TestCo</Company>
    <Copyright>Copyright © 2024</Copyright>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>

data.content:
Here's a summary of every legacy pattern identified and what was done:

| Legacy Pattern | Resolution |
|---|---|
| `ToolsVersion` + `xmlns` on `<Project>` | → `Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk"` |
| Explicit `Microsoft.Common.props` import | Removed (SDK handles it) |
| Explicit `Microsoft.CSharp.targets` import | Removed (SDK handles it) |
| `Configuration`/`Platform` conditions | Removed (SDK defaults) |
| `ProjectGuid` | Removed (not needed) |
| `OutputType Library` | Removed (default for SDK) |
| `RootNamespace`/`AssemblyName` | Removed (match project filename) |
| `TargetFrameworkVersion v4.7.2` | → `<TargetFramework>net472</TargetFramework>` |
| `FileAlignment`, `Deterministic` | Removed (SDK defaults) |
| `AutoGenerateBindingRedirects` | Removed (SDK default) |
| Debug/Release `PropertyGroup` blocks | Removed (SDK provides sensible defaults) |
| Explicit `<Reference>` for System assemblies | Removed (implicit for .NET Framework) |
| Explicit `<Compile Include="...">` items | Removed (SDK uses automatic globbing) |
| `Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs` | Deleted; metadata moved to `<PropertyGroup>` (`Description`, `Company`, `Copyright`, `Version`) |

The project went from **47 lines → 11 lines** while preserving all behavior.
